subject: ap-precalculus
title: Sinusoidal Modeling: Seasonal Temperature Variation
stimulus: The average daily high temperature in a city varies periodically throughout the year. The highest average temperature of $95^\circ$F occurs on day $201$ (July 20) and the lowest average temperature of $35^\circ$F occurs on day $20$ (January 20). Let $t$ represent the day of the year, where $t = 1$ is January 1. The sinusoidal function $W$ models the average daily high temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit, as a function of $t$.

parts.prompt:
The function $W$ can be written in the form $W(t) = a\sin(b(t - c)) + d$.

(i) Find the values of constants $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$.

(ii) Using the function $W$, find the average temperature predicted on day $t = 110$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Amplitude $a = (95-35)/2 = 30$; midline $d = (95+35)/2 = 65$; half-period $= 201 - 20 = 181$ days, so period $\approx 362$ (accept $365$); $b = 2\pi/365$ (accept $2\pi/362$). For the phase shift: in $W(t) = 30\sin(b(t-c)) + 65$, the maximum occurs when $b(t-c) = \pi/2$, i.e., $t = c + 1/(4 \cdot \text{period})$. With period $365$: $201 = c + 365/4 \approx c + 91.25$, so $c \approx 110$. Accept the cosine form $W(t) = -30\cos(b(t-20)) + 65$. For part (ii), $W(110) = 30\sin(0) + 65 = 65^\circ$F. This requires the $c \approx 110$ parameterization.

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 30$, $d = 65$, $b = \frac{2\pi}{365}$ (or $\frac{2\pi}{362}$), and $c \approx 110$ for the sine form (or $c = 20$ for the cosine form $-30\cos(b(t-20))+65$)
wrong_examples: $c = 201$ for sine form — this places maximum at $t = 201 + 365/4 \approx 292$, not $t = 201$
common_traps: In $a\sin(b(t-c)) + d$, the maximum of sine occurs when its argument equals $\pi/2$, NOT when $t = c$ — the phase shift $c$ is NOT the location of the maximum

scoring_points.description: Correct temperature $W(110) = 65^\circ$F using the parameterization from part (i); supporting work must show the substitution
wrong_examples: Answer inconsistent with the parameterization given in part (i)
common_traps: With $c = 110$, day $t = 110$ is at the midline crossing — the sinusoidal argument is $0$ and $\sin(0) = 0$, giving $W = d = 65^\circ$F

parts.prompt:
(i) Find all days $t$ where $1 \leq t \leq 365$ on which the model predicts an average temperature of exactly $65^\circ$F. Express answers as exact values.

(ii) On the interval $20 < t < 201$ (from the minimum to the maximum temperature), is $W$ increasing or decreasing? Describe the concavity of the graph of $W$ on this interval, noting any changes.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $W = 65$ gives $\sin(b(t-110)) = 0$, so $b(t-110) = k\pi$; solutions $t = 110 + 365k/2$. In $[1, 365]$: $k=0$ gives $t = 110$ and $k=1$ gives $t = 292.5$ (exact: $585/2$). Both required. For part (ii), $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$. Concavity changes at the midline crossing $t = 110$: concave up on $(20, 110)$ and concave down on $(110, 201)$. Noting the inflection at $t = 110$ is required — stating only 'concave up' or only 'concave down' does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Both correct days $t = 110$ and $t = 292.5$ (or $t = 585/2$); no incorrect values
wrong_examples: Including $t = 20$ or $t = 201$ (these are the min/max, not midline crossings)
common_traps: Two midline crossings per year — one ascending ($t = 110$) and one descending ($t = 292.5$)

scoring_points.description: $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$; concave up on $(20, 110)$ and concave down on $(110, 201)$, with inflection at the midline crossing $t = 110$

required_elements: concave up on $(20, 110)$ and concave down on $(110, 201)$
alternatives.correct_example: $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$. The graph is concave up on $(20, 110)$ (rate of increase is increasing) and concave down on $(110, 201)$ (rate of increase is decreasing). The inflection occurs at $t = 110$, the midline crossing.

wrong_examples: 'Always concave up' or 'always concave down' — incorrect, concavity changes at the midline
'Decreasing' on any part of $(20, 201)$ — incorrect, function increases from min to max
common_traps: The inflection point is at the midline crossing (not at the max or min) — concavity changes from up to down at $t = 110$

parts.prompt: A meteorologist claims that the model $W$ is equivalent to $W(t) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - c_0)\right) + 65$ for some value of $c_0$. Find the value of $c_0$ such that this cosine version has its minimum at $t = 20$. Show the trigonometric identity or reasoning used to connect the sine and cosine forms.
parts.scoring_notes: The simplest approach: $-30\cos(b(t-c_0))$ has minimum when $\cos = 1$, i.e., $b(t-c_0) = 0$, giving $t = c_0$. For minimum at $t = 20$: $c_0 = 20$. Accept $c_0 = 20$ with reasoning that 'minimum of $-\cos$ occurs when argument $= 0$.' Alternatively, using $\sin(\theta - \pi/2) = -\cos(\theta)$ links the sine form to the cosine form. Accept $c_0 = 18.75$ (exact) derived via identity. Both points can be earned through direct reasoning without identity.

scoring_points.description: Correct identity or reasoning connecting sine and cosine: either $\sin(\theta - \pi/2) = -\cos(\theta)$ applied to convert forms, OR direct reasoning that $-\cos(b(t-c_0))$ has minimum when $b(t-c_0) = 0$

required_elements: identity $-\cos\theta = \sin(\theta - \pi/2)$ OR reasoning: minimum of $-30\cos(b(t-c_0))$ when $\cos = 1$ i.e. $t = c_0$
alternatives.correct_example: Method 1: $-\cos\theta = \sin(\theta - \pi/2)$, so $-30\cos(b(t-c_0)) = 30\sin(b(t-c_0) - \pi/2) = 30\sin(b(t - c_0 - T/4))$. Method 2: $-30\cos(b(t-c_0))$ is minimized when $\cos(b(t-c_0)) = 1$, i.e., when $b(t-c_0) = 0$, i.e., when $t = c_0$.

wrong_examples: Claiming $c_0 = 201$ because the maximum is at $t = 201$ — confusing maximum of the function with the shift parameter
common_traps: $-30\cos$ achieves its minimum (not maximum) when $\cos = +1$ — a common confusion

scoring_points.description: Correct value $c_0 = 20$ (accept $c_0 = 18.75$ from identity approach); the minimum occurs at $t = c_0$ for the cosine form

alternatives.correct_example: For minimum at $t = 20$: $c_0 = 20$. The function $-30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-20)\right) + 65$ has minimum at $t = 20$ when the cosine argument equals $0$.

wrong_examples: $c_0 = 0$ (no phase shift — minimum would be at $t = 0$, not $t = 20$)
$c_0 = 201$ (this corresponds to the maximum, not minimum, location)
common_traps: The minimum of $-30\cos(b(t - c_0)) + 65$ occurs when $\cos(b(t-c_0)) = 1$, i.e., at $t = c_0$ — so $c_0 = 20$ places the minimum at day $20$
